---
title: "2.2 Boolean Expressions"
subtitle: "Selection and Iteration"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Conditions produce true or false

A <span class="term">Boolean expression</span> (布尔表达式) is an expression that evaluates to either `true` or `false`.

Boolean expressions are used by selection and iteration:

```java
if (score >= 90)
{
    System.out.println("A");
}
```

The code inside the block runs only when the condition is true.

## Testing Equality

### `==` compares primitive values

Use `==` to test whether two primitive values are equal.

```java
int age = 15;
System.out.println(age == 15);
System.out.println(age == 16);
```

Expected output:

```text
true
false
```

`=` assigns a value; `==` asks an equality question.

## Object References

### `==` with objects checks whether references point to the same object

![Turtle reference equality](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-2-boolean-expressions/turtleEquality.png){fig-align="center" width="43%"}

For reference types, `==` checks whether two variables refer to the same object, not whether two objects have equal-looking contents.

This distinction becomes important for `String` in Topic 2.6.

## Relational Operators

### Compare numeric values

| Operator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `<` | less than |
| `>` | greater than |
| `<=` | less than or equal to |
| `>=` | greater than or equal to |
| `==` | equal to |
| `!=` | not equal to |

Relational expressions also evaluate to `true` or `false`.

## Code Task

### Predict relational results

Predict the output before running.

```java
public class BooleanTrace
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int x = 3;
        int y = 5;
        System.out.println(x < y);
        System.out.println(x + 2 == y);
        System.out.println(y != 5);
        System.out.println(y >= x * 2);
    }
}
```

Expected output: `true`, `true`, `false`, `false`.

## Testing with Remainder

### `%` turns divisibility into a Boolean expression

The remainder operator is useful for number tests.

```java
int n = 17;
System.out.println(n % 2 == 0);
System.out.println(n % 5 == 2);
```

::: {.tight-list}
- `n % 2 == 0` tests whether `n` is even
- `n % divisor == 0` tests divisibility
- prime-number logic starts by checking possible divisors
:::

## Coding Challenge

### Prime Numbers POGIL starter

Complete the method by testing divisibility from `2` through `number - 1`.

```java
public class PrimeCheck
{
    public static boolean isPrime(int number)
    {
        if (number <= 1)
        {
            return false;
        }

        for (int divisor = 2; divisor < number; divisor++)
        {
            if (number % divisor == 0)
            {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

## Quick Check

### Prime reasoning

For `isPrime(9)`, which divisor first proves the number is not prime?

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `2`
- B. `3`
- C. `4`
- D. no divisor
:::

Answer: <span class="mark">B</span>. `9 % 3 == 0`, so `9` is not prime.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain that a Boolean expression evaluates to `true` or `false`
- distinguish assignment `=` from equality testing `==`
- use relational operators correctly with numeric values
- explain how `%` supports even, odd, divisibility, and prime checks
- avoid assuming object reference equality means object content equality
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
